coredata.dc:title: Preparation of gadolinium doped carbon dots for enhanced MR imaging and cell fluorescence labeling
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Shaohui
Yu, Nana
Han, Cuiping
Xie, Ting
Dou, Binru
Kong, Ying
Zuo, Fengmei
Shi, Meilin
Xu, Kai
coredata.dc:description: Gadolinium doped carbon dots (Gd-CDs) were prepared as a dual-modal imaging agent for enhanced MR imaging and cell fluorescence imaging. The Gd-CDs were synthesized via one-step solvent free technique with Gd-DTPA and l-arginine as the Gd and carbon sources with a quantum yield of 57.78%. The Gd-CDs exhibited good crystal structure, excellent aqueous dispersity, high colloidal stability, intense fluorescence and low cytotoxicity. The bio-TEM images revealed that the Gd-CDs could be easily internalized by cancer cells and escape from the endosomes. Furthermore, the Gd-CDs demonstrated wonderful multi-color fluoresence cell labeling ability at various excitation wavelength and much better MR contrast effect compared with commercial Gd-DTPA with a high r1 relaxivity value 6.27 mM−1s−1. In addition, Gd-CDs exhibited brighter MR signal than Gd-DTPA in the animal MR imaging test. Finally, the Gd-CDs also indicated low long-term toxicity by the serum biochemistry analysis. Thus, these results indicated that Gd-CDs would be an excellent dual-modal imaging probe for enhanced MR imaging and fluorescence imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gadolinium
Carbon dots
Dual-modal contrast agent
Fluorescence imaging
Enhanced magnetic resonance imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro cell cytotoxicity and cellular uptake
In vitro cytotoxicity and cellular uptake of Gd-CDs
In vitro bioimaging and cell labeling

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 81671744 , 81771904 , 81602533 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project ( 2016M591923 , 2017T100405 ), Jiangsu Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project ( 1601189C ), the project of Invigorating Health Care through Science, Technology and Education, Jiangsu Provincial Medical Youth Talent ( QNRC2016782 ), Xuzhou innovation project (Grant NO. KC16SY153 ), and sponsored by Qing Lan Project . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of Gd-CDs: (A) TEM images of Gd-CDs, (inset, high resolution TEM images), (B) Size distributions of Gd-CDs; (B) UV–visible absorption of the Gd-CDs, inset pictures show the Gd-CDs under natural light and UV light, (D) The PL emission of the Gd-CDs with excitation wavelengths from 330 nm to 400 nm in 10 nm increments. XPS spectrum of Gd-CDs: (E) Full-scan spectrum, (F) Gd4d spectrum.

(A) In vitro cytotoxicity test of Gd-CDs. (B) Cellular uptake test with Bio-TEM of Gd-CDs by 786-O cells for (a) 2 h and (b) 4 h. (scale bar = 2 μm), (C) Multi-color CLSM imaging of 786-O cells and HO-8910 cells labeled with Gd-CDs (scale bar = 20 μm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

MR contrast ability of Gd-CDs. (A) Linear relationship between longitudinal relaxation rate of Gd-CDs and Gd-DTPA at different concentrations. (B) T 1-weighted phantom images of Gd-CDs and Gd-DTPA at different Gd concentrations using a 3.0 T MR scanner. (C) T 1-weighted 786-O cellular MR imaging of Gd-CDs and Gd-DTPA at various concentrations. (D) T 1-weighted 786-O cellular MR imaging signal intensity of Gd-CDs and Gd-DTPA at various concentrations.

T 1-weighted in vivo MR imaging of mice before (plain scanning, P.S) and after injection of Gd-CDs and Gd-DTPA at different time points (0.5, 1, 2, 6, 12 h): (A) MR images, (B) Signal intensity in the kidneys; (C) Mouse serum biochemistry analysis before (0d, control) and after injection of Gd-CDs and Gd-DTPA for 1, 7, 21d.

head.ce:title: Preparation of gadolinium doped carbon dots for enhanced MR imaging and cell fluorescence labeling

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Medical Imaging and Digital Medicine, School of Medical Imaging, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, 221004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Medical Imaging and Digital Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Radiology, Affiliated Hospital of Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, 221004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Affiliated Hospital of Xuzhou Medical University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Institute of Medical Imaging and Digital Medicine, School of Medical Imaging, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, 221004, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Medical Imaging and Digital Medicine

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Institute of Medical Imaging and Digital Medicine, School of Medical Imaging, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, 221004, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Medical Imaging and Digital Medicine

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Shaohui Zheng, Nana Yu and Cuiping Han contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Gadolinium doped carbon dots (Gd-CDs) were prepared as a dual-modal imaging agent for enhanced MR imaging and cell fluorescence imaging. The Gd-CDs were synthesized via one-step solvent free technique with Gd-DTPA and l-arginine as the Gd and carbon sources with a quantum yield of 57.78%. The Gd-CDs exhibited good crystal structure, excellent aqueous dispersity, high colloidal stability, intense fluorescence and low cytotoxicity. The bio-TEM images revealed that the Gd-CDs could be easily internalized by cancer cells and escape from the endosomes. Furthermore, the Gd-CDs demonstrated wonderful multi-color fluoresence cell labeling ability at various excitation wavelength and much better MR contrast effect compared with commercial Gd-DTPA with a high r1 relaxivity value 6.27 mM−1s−1. In addition, Gd-CDs exhibited brighter MR signal than Gd-DTPA in the animal MR imaging test. Finally, the Gd-CDs also indicated low long-term toxicity by the serum biochemistry analysis. Thus, these results indicated that Gd-CDs would be an excellent dual-modal imaging probe for enhanced MR imaging and fluorescence imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, multi-modal imaging has emerged as an effective diagnosis technique to acquire comprehensive information of the target tissues by combining two or more imaging methods, including fluorescence imaging, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), photoacoustic imaging (PA), computed tomography imaging (CT) and position emission tomography (PET) [1–5]. Among them, the combination of fluorescence imaging and MRI provides effective complementary set in imaging attributed their different imaging properties [2,6]. MR imaging is one of the most powerful imaging techniques attributed to its high 3D spatial resolution and deep tissue penetration [2,3,7,8]. On the other hand, fluorescence imaging provides high sensitivity to label and detect the diseases in subcellular levels inside the target tissues, which could compensate the limits of MRI [9]. Therefore, the FL/MR dual-modal imaging enables it to be a promising technique to acquire more comprehensive information in cancer diagnosis ascribed to their complementary characteristics of the two imaging modalities [10]. As one of the T 1 weighted contrast agent, gadolinium (Gd) exhibited excellent contrast effect in MR imaging attributed its inherent physical and chemical properties [11]. However, the surface of Gd is needed to be modified to formulate it in aqueous solution and reduce its biological toxicity caused by Gd ions leakage. Typically, Gd was embedded inside the nano-vesicles to deal with the aforementioned problems, including liposomes, micelles, metallic nanoparticles and carbon nanomaterials, etc [10–13].

Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted great attention as a novel fluorescent nanomaterial attributed to their ultrasmall size, good water solubility, remarkable optical properties and low toxicity [14]. Encouraged by their excellent properties, CDs exhibited great potential in a wide range of biomedical applications, including bioimaging, cell labeling, bio-sensing, drug delivery, etc [15–17]. Furthermore, the CDs also revealed bright up-conversion photoluminescence and multi-color emission ascribed to the surface modification with organic or biomolecules, enabling the application for bioimaging [17]. For example, Mehta et al. prepared multicolor emitting carbon dots and successfully applied in Hela cell imaging at various color channels [17]. Liu et al. successfully synthesized nitrogen-doped conjugated carbonized polymer dots with 31% efficient red emission for in vivo imaging [18].

Herein, in this study, we have developed a simple one-step solvent free method to synthesize Gd-doped CDs for enhanced MR imaging and cell fluorescence labeling. The composition and structure of Gd-CDs were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction analysis and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). The optical properties were analyzed with UV–vis spectrometer and the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra. The pathway of cellular uptake was analyzed by Bio-TEM. Encouraged by the excellent optical properties, we investigated the potential of application in cell fluorescence imaging using the Gd-CDs. Enhanced contrast effect of imaging was assessed by the in vitro MR cancer cell imaging and in vivo MR imaging with Gd-CDs and Gd-DTPA incubation. Finally, the long-term toxicity of Gd-CDs was investigated by the serum biochemistry analysis. Therefore, the prepared Gd-CDs would be a promising candidate in dual-modal imaging for cancer diagnosis.

Gadopentetic acid (Magnevist, Gd-DTPA) was supplied by Bayer Pharma AG (German). l-arginine was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide (MTT) was received from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA). Dulbecco's Minimum Essential Medium (DMEM), Roswell Park Memorial Institute 1640 Medium (RPMI-1640), fetal bovine serum (FBS), EDTA-trypsin, and penicillin/streptomycin were purchased from Gibco BRL (MD, USA). All other chemicals were used in this study as received without further purification.

Gd-doped carbon dots were synthesized via one-step solvent free method. Briefly, 100 μL Gd-DTPA was firstly diluted in 100 μL water, then was mixed with 0.1 mmol l-arginine under stirring to form homogeneous solution. Thereafter, the mixing solution was heated at 200 °C for 1.5 h. Subsequently, the reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature and re-dispersed in water and precipitated with 3 vol of acetone and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. Then the product was freeze-dried to obtain power and stored under 4 °C.

The quantum yield (QY) of prepared Gd-CDs was assessed by referring the standard QY value of quinine sulfate solution in H2SO4. Briefly, the Gd-CDs were firstly dissolved in DI water at various concentrations. The absorbance of the samples was recorded using a UV–vis spectrometer and the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of the samples were measured by a LS55 Fluorescence Spectrometer at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Then, the samples were analyzed with the PL spectrometer and PL emission intensity of excitation wavelength at which the Gd-CDs and the standard showed the same absorbance. The QY could be calculated using the following equation: QYx = QYs × [As/Ax] × [Ix/Is], where QYs is the QY of quinine sulphate, As and Ax are the maximum absorption intensities of the quinine sulfate and Gd-CDs, respectively, and I is the area under the fluorescence curve.

The size, morphology and composition of prepared Gd-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy with energy-dispersive X-ray function (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 Spirit). The size distribution and zeta potential were measured by a DSL analyzer (Zetasizer Nano ZS90). An UV–vis–NIR spectrometer (UH4150 HITACH) was used to record the UV–vis absorption spectra of the Gd-CDs. The PL emission measurements were performed on a LS55 Fluorescence Spectrometer (PerkinElmer, America). The crystal structure of Gd-CDs was assessed by X-ray diffraction patterns with a Rigaku diffracctometer. The chemical structure of Gd-CDs was characterized via a Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Bruker Tensor27). The elemental composition of Gd-CDs was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo escalab250Xi, USA). The content of gadolinium in Gd-CDs was quantified by inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-MS, Thermal Elemental).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cell cytotoxicity and cellular uptake

ce:section.ce:para: 786-O human renal cancer cells, HK-2 tubular epithelial cells and HO-8910 human ovarian cancer cells were obtained from American Type Culture Collection (ATCC). The above-mentioned cell lines were maintained in RPMI 1640 and DMEM medium, respectively, containing 10% (v/v) FBS and 1% penicillin-streptomycin and incubated in 37 °C in a humidified 5% CO2 atmosphere.

The cell cytotoxicity of the Gd-CDs was investigated by the standard MTT assay [19]. Briefly, the 786-O and HK-2 cells were seeded in 96 well-plate and incubated 24 h to allow cell attachment. Then, Gd-CDs at different concentrations were added in each well and maintained for another 24 h. Afterwards, MTT solution (10 μL, 5 mg/mL in PBS) was added to each well in all plates and incubated for another 4 h. Subsequently, the medium was carefully removed and DMSO (100 μL) was added to dissolve the formazan crystals. Finally, the absorbance was measured using a microplate reader (BioTek Epoch, Service Card). The cell viability was expressed as a percentage of surviving cells as the mean values of triplicate measurements.

Bio-TEM was used to investigate the cellular uptake of Gd-CDs by 786-O renal cancer cells to reveal the endocytosis and intracellular location of the carbon dots [20]. Briefly, the cells were seeded on 12-well plates at a density of 105 cells per well for 24 h, then were treated by Gd-CDs for 2 h and 4 h for cellular uptake. Afterwards, the cells were harvested and centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 1 min, following with the fixation by 2.5% glutaraldehyde solution for 1 h. The fixed samples were then washed with PBS solution and dehydrate with graded ethanol and propylene oxide. Thereafter, the sample were embedded in EPSM812 and polymerized in the oven at 37 °C overnight. Ultrathin sections of the cells were cut and observed by a Bio-TEM.

The 786-O and HO-8910 cancer cells were used to evaluate the potential of Gd-CDs as fluorescence cell labeling agent. Cells were seeded on sterile coverslips in a 12-well plate overnight for cell attachment. Then, the cells were incubated with Gd-CDs for 4 h at 0.1 mg/mL. Subsequently, the cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10 min and washed thrice with cold PBS. Finally, the samples were observed using a confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, LSM510, Zeiss, Germany).

The T 1-weighted relaxation study of Gd-CDs was performed on a 3.0 T Discovery 750 W MR system. Briefly, the Gd-CDs and Gd-DTPA were diluted in PBS at Gd concentrations of 0.46, 0.91, 1.37, 1.82, 2.28, 2.73 and 3.18 mM. The MRI of these diluted samples was performed with Spin-Echo sequence. The longitudinal and transverse relaxation times of these samples were detected and deployed to calculate the relaxation rate. The T 1-weighted MR phantom images were obtained under the following parameters: TR = 425 ms, TE = Min Full, Matrix size = 384 × 224, Field of View = 18 cm × 18 cm, Slice thickness = 3.0 mm, spacing = 1.5 mm.

To evaluate the in vitro enhanced MR imaging ability of Gd-CDs, the cells were seeded on 6-well plate for 24 h. Then, the cells were treated with Gd-DTPA and Gd-CDs at various concentrations (0, 0.5, 1, 3 mg/mL) for 2 h to allow the cellular uptake. Afterwards, the cells were washed thrice with cold PBS and trypsinized with EDTA-trypsin, then were re-suspended in 200 μL 1% agarose gel. Finally, the MR imaging was performed using previously described MR system.

Animal experiments were performed by the approval of Animal Care Committee of Xuzhou Medical University. Kunming mice were used to investigate the potential of Gd-CDs in MR imaging. T 1-weighted MR imaging were acquired before and after injection (0.5, 1, 2, 6, 12 h) of Gd-DTPA and Gd-CDs (Gd concentration = 5 mg/kg). The SE sequence parameters were set as follows: TR = 16 ms, TE = 3.2 ms, Field of View = 8 cm × 8 cm, Slice thickness = 0.4 mm, spacing = 0.5 mm, Matrix size = 320 × 288.

Kunming mice (female, 8 weeks) were randomly divided into 3 groups. The mice were injected via tail vein with PBS, Gd-DTPA and Gd-CDs (Gd concentration = 5 mg/kg). The blood samples were collected on day 1, 7, 21 days (n = 5) for blood serum analysis. Then, the blood samples were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 5min to obtain the serum for biochemistry analysis.

Gd-CDs were synthesized via one-step hydrothermal method using Gd-DTPA and l-arginine as the gadolinium and carbon source, respectively. As shown in the TEM image (Fig. 1 A), the Gd-CDs exhibited uniform spherical morphology and excellent monodispersity with an average particle size of approximately 5.38 nm and relatively narrow size distribution. The high resolution examination of TEM images indicated that the Gd-CDs were well crystalline with noticeable lattice fringes (Fig. 1A(inset)). Moreover, the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis of Gd-CDs indicated the presence of Gd element in the Gd-CDs (Fig. S1). The content of Gd in Gd-CDs was further estimated by ICP-MS, and found to be almost 12% (wt %), which is sufficient for further T 1-weighted MR imaging.

The chemical structure and composition of the prepared Gd-CDs was investigated by FTIR and XPS analysis. As shown in the FTIR spectra (Fig. S2), it can be figured that the peak emerged at 3363 cm−1 corresponds to the OH and NH stretching vibrations of amine groups, 2978 cm−1 for CH stretching vibrations, 1601 cm−1 for CO stretching vibrations, 1403 cm−1 for CC stretching vibrations, 1317 cm−1 for CN stretching vibrations and 1035 cm−1 for CH stretching vibrations. The FTIR result indicated the presence of typical absorption bands of Gd-CDs. The successful synthesis of Gd-CDs was further investigated by XPS analysis. As shown in the XPS spectra of Gd-CDs (Fig. 1E), the Gd-CDs mainly composed of C, N, O, as well as the Gd elements. Two typical binding energies were observed at 1187 and 1219 eV, which correspond to the spin-orbit coupled Gd (3d5/2) and Gd (3d3/2) that is consistent to the Gd3+ in Gd2O3, respectively. As shown in the high resolution spectra of Gd4d (Fig. 1F), two peaks presented at 147.6 and 142.1 eV, which agreed with the spectra of Gd (2p3/2) and Gd (2p5/2) in Gd-DTPA, indicating that part of Gd3+ did not significantly change their chemical environment after Gd-CDs formation. Moreover, there were also three strong binding energies observed at 284, 398 and 530 eV, which represents the typical peaks of C1S, N1S and O1S, respectively. Particularly, the C1S spectra revealed three peaks at 284.3, 286.8 and 288.8 eV, demonstrating the presence of CC, CN and CO, CO bond, respectively (Figure. S3A). Furthermore, the high resolution spectrum of O1S also approved the existence of CO and CO bond at 531.2 eV and 530.1 eV (Fig. S3B). Therefore, the above results from multiple perspectives proved the successful synthesis of Gd-CDs.

Furthermore, the optical properties of Gd-CDs were investigated using UV/Vis absorption and photo-luminescence (PL) spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1C, the absorbance of Gd-CDs exhibited no noticeable absorption peak along the whole range (200–600 nm) in aqueous condition. Moreover, as shown in the inset figure of Figure .1C(inset), the Gd-CDs aqueous solution was observed as light yellow and transparent under natural light, but turned to bright blue under UV light excitation. The quantum yield of Gd-CDs was investigated by comparing with quinine sulfate solution in 0.1 M H2SO4, and was estimated to be 57.78 ± 2.14%. As shown in Fig. 1D and Fig. S4, the PL spectra of the Gd-CDs revealed broad range of emission wavelength and a typical excitation wavelength-dependent characteristic, which the maximum emission peak red shifts in the 380–470 nm range as the excitation wavelength range from 330 to 400 nm. Therefore, the results demonstrated that the Gd-CDs possessed excellent fluorescent properties for cell imaging.

Moreover, Gd-CDs showed excellent colloidal dispersity in different solutions including DI water, PBS, FBS medium, RPMI-1640 medium and DMEM medium, which would guarantee a long circulation time in the blood system (Fig. S5). Moreover, the Gd-CDs also revealed similar high T 1-weighted MR signal in the abovemetioned media, indicating their high stability as MR contrast agent in various media (Fig. S6). In addition, a 7-day period particle size change was also investigated and was found that no noticeable size change was measured during the 7 day-peroid, demonstrating the long term stability of Gd-CDs in aqueous condition (Fig. S7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity and cellular uptake of Gd-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The biocompatibility is of great importance for Gd-CDs to be used for MR imaging and cell labeling. The cytotoxicity of Gd-CDs at various concentrations was assessed against 786-O renal cancer cells and HK-2 tubular epithelial cells using MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the cell viabilities of 786-O and HK-2 cells remained more than 90% after 24 h even at a high concentration of 1 mg/mL, suggesting the low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility of the Gd-CDs to the cells.

The uptake and intracellular location of Gd-CDs in the living 786-O cells was investigated using Bio-TEM. As shown in Fig. 2B (a), the Gd-CDs were largely phagocytized by 786-O cancer cells and entrapped inside the intracellular endosome. Thereafter, the Gd-CDs escaped out of the endosomes and mainly distributed in the cytoplasm (Fig. 2B (b)). The result suggested that the Gd-CDs could be readily taken up and internalized by the renal cancer cells and escaped from the endosome to distribute in the cytoplasm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro bioimaging and cell labeling
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the capability of Gd-CDs for bioimaging and cell labeling, in vitro cellular imaging using Gd-CDs was investigated on 786-O and HO-8910 cells by CLSM and illustrated in Fig. 2C. CLSM images revealed multicolor emission under various excitation wavelengths in two cancer cell lines: blue, green and red, which could provide more precise information in cellular imaging compared with single fluorescence. The multi-color emission agrees with the excitation-dependent emission property of prepared Gd-CDs shown in Fig. 1D. The CLSM images indicated that the Gd-CDs could be internalized inside both 786-O and HO-8910 cancer cells via the endocytosis process, which was verified in section 3.2. Moreover, the Gd-CDs were mainly distributed in the cytoplasm, which was consistent with the result observed by Bio-TEM. More importantly, the cells maintained their living morphology during the test, indicating the low damage of Gd-CDs to the cell. Therefore, the Gd-CDs could be a potential agent for bioimaging and cell labeling.

T 1-weighted MR phantom study was performed to assess the feasibility of Gd-CDs as a new MR contrast agent in comparison with the commercial product Gd-DTPA. The MR phantom images were conducted by a previously described MR system. As shown in phantom images and pseudo-color images (Fig. 3 B), the intensity of MR signal of Gd-CDs and Gd-DTPA increased along with the increase of Gd concentrations, demonstrating that the prepared Gd-CDs could be applied as new candidate for T 1-weighted MR imaging as Gd-DTPA. Particularly, the transverse relaxation rate (1/T 1) exhibited a linear relation with the Gd concentrations, where the slope was defined as T 1 relaxivity (r1, mM−1s−1). As shown in Fig. 3A, the r1 value of Gd-CDs was calculated to be 6.27 mM−1s−1, which was 1.4-fold comparing with the value of Gd-DTPA (4.49 mM−1s−1), indicating an enhanced MR contrast ability.

Furthermore, the in vitro MR imaging test on 786-O cancer cells was used to evaluate the ability of prepared Gd-CDs for cancer cell imaging. The 786-O cells were co-incubated with Gd-CDs and Gd-DTPA for 4 h and scanned by the MR system. As shown in Fig. 3C, the Gd-CDs treated group exhibited much brighter images compared with the groups treated with Gd-DTPA. In addition, the signal intensity of Gd-CDs groups was almost 2-fold of the intensity of Gd-DTPA groups at the same Gd concentration (Fig. 3D), indicating that the Gd-CDs have a better contrast ability in T 1-weighted MR cell imaging. The results demonstrated that the Gd-CDs could serve as an MR contrast agent for cancer imaging and enhance the relaxivity of the commercial product.

Animal tests were conducted to investigate the potential of Gd-CDs for in vivo MR imaging. The T 1-weighted MR images were obtained at different time points after injection of Gd-CDs and Gd-DTPA. As shown in Fig. 4 A and B, compared with the plain scanning images, both Gd-CDs and Gd-DTPA revealed bright signal in the target organ kidney after administration for 30min. For Gd-DTPA injected mice, the MR signal increased to the peak within 30 min and then significantly declined to low signal in 2 h. In contrast, for Gd-CDs group, the MR signal exhibited excellent contrast effect after 30min and maintained high signal even after 12 h injection. More importantly, the Gd-CDs group exhibited much stronger MR signal than Gd-DTPA group at most of the test period. These results demonstrated the potential of Gd-CDs as effective T 1-weighted MR contrast agent for in vivo imaging.

To assess the potential toxicity of Gd-CDs and Gd-DTPA, serum biochemistry analysis was carried out. As shown in Fig. 4C, the Gd-CDs exhibited similar effect to the animals compared with the Gd-DTPA. Separately, the kidney indicators blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and creatinine revealed no significant difference with the control group, indicating the Gd-CDs induced no damage to the renal function. However, the aspartate aminotransferase (AST) and alanine aminotransferase (ALT) values for hepatic function revealed slight increase after 1-d injection and returned back to the normal level in 7 and 21-d as the control group. Moreover, the other hepatic indicators-albumin (ALB) and total protein (TP) maintained in the normal level during whole test period. It indicated that the Gd-CDs and Gd-DTPA may affect the hepatic function shortly after the injection without significant damage to the hepatic tissues. The liver could quickly recover its function after 7days injection. Furthermore, all the indicators were within the normal ranges during the experiment. These results demonstrated the low toxicity of Gd-CDs and Gd-DTPA to the animals in a long term.

In summary, we successfully prepared the Gd-doped carbon dots via the one-step hydrothermal technique with Gd-DTPA and l-arginine for enhanced MR imaging and cell labeling. Gadolinium ions were successfully doped inside the carbons dots with a high loading efficiency of 12%. The Gd-CDs exhibited excellent aqueous dispersity, high colloidal stability, intense fluorescence and low cytotoxicity. Moreover, the Gd-CDs could be readily internalized by cells and revealed wonderful multi-fluorescence cell labeling ability at various excitation wavelength. Furthermore, in the in vitro and in vivo MR test, the Gd-CDs exhibited much better MR contrast effect compared with commercial Gd-DTPA product with a high r1 relaxivity value 6.27 mM−1s−1. Furthermore, the Gd-CDs also exhibited low long-term toxicity from the serum biochemistry analysis. Therefore, we anticipate that the prepared Gd-CDs could serve as a new dual-modal probe for enhanced MR imaging and cell fluorescence labeling.
There are no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81671744, 81771904, 81602533), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project (2016M591923, 2017T100405), Jiangsu Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project (1601189C), the project of Invigorating Health Care through Science, Technology and Education, Jiangsu Provincial Medical Youth Talent (QNRC2016782), Xuzhou innovation project (Grant NO. KC16SY153), and sponsored by Qing Lan Project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
supplementary data-Gd-CDs supplementary data-Gd-CDs

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bbrc.2019.01.098.
